coredata.dc:title: Understanding the aggregation of excitation wavelength independent emission of amphiphilic carbon dots for bioimaging and organic acid sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Prakash, Swayam
Sahu, Saugata
Patra, Bamadeb
Mishra, Ashok Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Herein, excitation wavelength-independent, tunable emissive and amphiphilic CDs with high quantum yield were synthesized by a low-temperature oxidation method employing banana peel waste as a carbon source. These CDs showed longer wavelength emissions (green to yellow) independent of the excitation wavelength when dispersed in different polar to non-polar solvents. The quantum yields of the same CDs were 9–32% in different solvent polarities for different emissions. On the other hand, a large stokes-shifted emission (∼9606 cm−1) was observed for CDs in the non-polar and weak polar solvents. The particle size of CDs increases from a hydrophobic to a hydrophilic environment with the change in emission colour from yellow to green. A polar and a non-polar host matrix were used to overcome the limitation of aggregation-caused quenching of CDs in the solid state to obtain bright emissions. These CDs were potentially used for naked-eye detection of trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) by changing the emission colour from yellow to orange under UV 365 nm. Sensing of TFA was also shown reversibly switch emission colour and average lifetime for multiple cycles. Additionally, the highly emissive CDs show negligible cytotoxicity in 3T3 fibroblast cells, indicating possible bioimaging applications in 3T3 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Excitation wavelength independent emission of carbon dots
Sensing hydrophilic and hydrophobic solvent
Dual emission
Sensing organic acid
Bioimaging application
Amphiphilic carbon dots
CDs-NaCl crystal
CDs-PMMA
Solid-state fluorescent carbon dots
Naked-eye TFA detection
Aggregation of carbon dots
Solvatochromism of carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell Culture, cytotoxicity assay and fluorescence imaging
Photophysics of excitation wavelength independent emissive CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Department of Chemistry and SAIF, IIT Madras, for the instrumental facilities. The authors thank Dr E. Prasad, Department of Chemistry, IIT Madras, for providing the DLS instrument facility. The authors thank IIT Madras for their research fellowship. SP grateful to Dr Anand Kumar Sahu for the proofreading.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image of CDs in dichloromethane; inset shows lattice fringes d = 0.35 nm, (B) XRD patterns of CDs, (C) EDAX graph of CDs and (D) FTIR spectrum of CDs.

(A) Absorption spectra of CDs in different solvents, (B) Emission spectras of CDs in different solvents with excitation of 360 nm, (C) Emission spectras of CDs in different solvents with excitation of 440 nm, (D) Correlation of emission maxima with solvent polarity index at excitation of 360 nm and 440 nm and (E) Digital picture of CDs in different solvents in daylight and under UV 365 nm.
Excitation spectra of CDs were recorded at different emission wavelengths (λ) in (A) dichloromethane, (B) ethyl acetate, (C) dimethyl sulfoxide, (D) 1-butanol, (E) methanol and (F) water.
Size distribution of CDs in different solvents.

(A) Schematic presentation of aggregation cause of quenching (ACQ) and the strategy for suppressing ACQ by embedding CDs into ionic and polymer host matrix, (B)EEMF graph of CDs-NaCl crystals, (C) EEMF graph of CDs in the solid-state, (D) EEMF graph of CDs-PMMA, (E) Steady-state absorbance and emission spectra of CDs-NaCl and CDs-PMMA and (F) digital picture of CDs-NaCl crystals, CDs in the solid-state and CDs-PMMA under UV 365 nm and (G) Time-resolved spectra of CDs-NaCl and CDs-PMMA.

(A) Change in absorbance of CDs with increase in the concentration of TFA 4.3 μM to 2.1 mM, (B) Steady-state emission intensity ratio (I455/I560) and (I500/I590) of CDs with vary in TFA concentration; λex = 360 nm and λex = 430 nm, (C) Normalized emission intensity of CDs with the increase in the concentration of TFA 4.3 μM to 2.1 mM at λex = 360 nm and (D) Comparative bar plot of emission intensity of CDs in presence of various organic acids at λex = 360 nm and λem = 560 nm. The abbreviation TFA stands for trifluoroacetic acid, and TEA stands for triethylamine.

(A) Normalized fluorescence intensity of CDs with the simultaneous mixing of equal concentration (200 μM) of TFA followed by TEA up to several cycles, (B) Time-resolved decay of CDs with the simultaneous mixing of equal concentration (200 μM) of TFA followed by TEA up to several cycles, (C) Change in emission intensity (λex/ λem = 360/560 nm) and average lifetime (λex/ λem = 350/560 nm) with the addition of TFA followed by TEA up to several cycles and (D) Change of emission wavelength with the excitation of 360 nm by the addition of TFA followed by TEA up to several cycles. The abbreviation TFA stands for trifluoroacetic acid, and TEA stands for triethylamine.

(A) Relative viability of 3T3 fibroblast cells treated with different concentrations of CDs (Control experiment of CDs had absorption value at 360 nm = 0.7. Different concentrations of CDs were achieved with different dilution factors) and (B) Fluorescence microscope image of 3T3 fibroblast cells with the presence of CDs (scale bar 100 µm).
Synthesis methodology for the formation of excitation wavelength independent emissive CDs.
Emission lifetime (τ, ns), average lifetime (τavg, ns). The relative amplitudes are provided in parathesis. The error in the measurement = ±5%.

Comparison of quantum yield of CDs in various solvents. Emission quantum yield (Φ), radiative rate constant (kr/108, s−1) and non-radiative rate constant (knr/108, s−1) of CDs in different solvents. The perylene was used as a standard reference (Φ = 0.94 in cyclohexane) and excited at 362 nm and 419 nm.[49].

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: and b represent the quantum yield measurement at λex = 362 nm and λex = 419 nm, respectively.

head.ce:title: Understanding the aggregation of excitation wavelength independent emission of amphiphilic carbon dots for bioimaging and organic acid sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai 600036, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai 600036, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai 600036, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai 600036, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An insight into the solvatochromic and photophysical properties of excitation wavelength independent emission of amphiphilic carbon dots for bioimaging and organic acid sensing application.

Herein, excitation wavelength-independent, tunable emissive and amphiphilic CDs with high quantum yield were synthesized by a low-temperature oxidation method employing banana peel waste as a carbon source. These CDs showed longer wavelength emissions (green to yellow) independent of the excitation wavelength when dispersed in different polar to non-polar solvents. The quantum yields of the same CDs were 9–32% in different solvent polarities for different emissions. On the other hand, a large stokes-shifted emission (∼9606 cm−1) was observed for CDs in the non-polar and weak polar solvents. The particle size of CDs increases from a hydrophobic to a hydrophilic environment with the change in emission colour from yellow to green. A polar and a non-polar host matrix were used to overcome the limitation of aggregation-caused quenching of CDs in the solid state to obtain bright emissions. These CDs were potentially used for naked-eye detection of trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) by changing the emission colour from yellow to orange under UV 365 nm. Sensing of TFA was also shown reversibly switch emission colour and average lifetime for multiple cycles. Additionally, the highly emissive CDs show negligible cytotoxicity in 3T3 fibroblast cells, indicating possible bioimaging applications in 3T3 cells.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Excitation wavelength independent emission of carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The unique properties of carbon dots (CDs), i.e. zero-dimensional, low cytotoxicity, low cost, chemical inertness, low photo-bleaching, and excellent biocompatibility, have been utilized for alternative use of fluorescent dyes for bioimaging, drug delivery and many more applications [1–6]. Usually, CDs emit in the visible range with excitation wavelength-dependent emission in most cases [4,7]. The excitation-dependent nature arises due to the distinct photon absorption mechanism from blue to red regions. They often show very low quantum yield [8]. On the other hand, CDs have limited biological applications due to their short wavelength emission [8]. These limitations severely restrict the bioimaging applications of CDs. As a result, developing CDs with an excitation wavelength-independent longer wavelength emission and high emission quantum yield is highly pertinent for many potential applications. In the future, excitation wavelength-independent, emissive CDs will be a very good substitute instead of organic dyes for easy and facile synthesis.

Currently, attempts are being conducted to create blue-to-red emissive CDs with an excitation wavelength-independent nature [8–14]. However, these synthesis methods of CDs involved pyrolysis, hydrothermal or solvothermal with long reaction times at very high temperatures 200 °C to 500 °C followed by post-purification methods (dialysis or column chromatography etc.) [8–14]. Importantly, these excitation wavelengths independent CDs had a few more limitations, i.e. (i) low quantum yield, (ii) CDs are either hydrophilic or hydrophobic, and (iii) quenching of fluorescence in the solid state. Additionally, amphiphilic multicolour CDs are less frequently reported [15,16], limiting their potential applications in a variety of fields [17–19]. Most literature publications discuss excitation wavelength-dependent emission maxima for CDs due to heterogeneity. However, the crucial concern is how homogenous particles can be produced in a facile approach which can produce excitation wavelength independent emission of CDs with amphiphilic nature. Therefore, it is essential to design amphiphilic excitation wavelength independent CDs with tunable emission nature. Furthermore, this study will enable us to understand CDs fluorescence mechanisms with their novel applications.

Notably, the origin of fluorescence from CDs is poorly understood due to the complicated molecular structure and the intrinsic mechanisms governing the fluorescence origin [20–22]. Excitation dependent multicolour emissive CDs have often been derived from the size of their different nanoparticles, which involve many emission states or trap states [1,15,23,24]. The multicolour luminescence of CDs, in fact, exhibits the bandgap transitions assigned to conjugated sp2-domains (carbon core) [22,25,26]. The bandgap of CDs may be controlled by varying the size of conjugated sp2-domains, resulting in different emission colours [22,25,26]. On the other hand, it has also been observed that O-, N-, P- and S-related defect states on the surface of CDs contribute to the colour-tunable emission [27]. The characterization and interpretation of CDs fluorescence are complicated by mainly two factors: (i) variation in excitation wavelength and (ii) solvent nature [22,23,28–31]. Some researchers describe this as a quantum confinement effect and state that a heterogeneous core size distribution creates a variety of emission wavelengths [22,32]. On the other hand, few studies interpret it in terms of surface fluorophore emission [22]. The incomplete carbonization of CDs results from a combination of various molecules, oligomers, polymer chains, polymer clusters, and carbon cores [33 34,35]. Hence CDs have far more complicated structures and components than expected, resulting in more complex photoluminescence origins. As a result, understanding the complex origin of the fluorescence behaviour of CDs remains a significant challenge. This prompted us to conduct the current research, in which the excitation wavelength independent emission properties of CDs were designed and investigated by their photophysical prospective.

In one of our previous studies, we adopted a facile room-temperature synthesis method for CDs and CDs-NaCl crystals formation from banana peel waste [4]. The ripe banana peel was strategically chosen as a source of CDs formation. The ripe banana peels are rich in simple sugars (glucose, fructose and sucrose), i.e. mainly fructose, which facilitates CDs formation [36,37]. Our earlier work showed that the emission behaviour of CDs and CD-NaCl crystals had excitation wavelength dependent emission with low quantum yield [4]. However, the CD-NaCl crystals show very good applications in Fabry-Perot mode lasing and white light generation in solid-state [4]. Herein, we developed a strategy to get excitation wavelength independent emission of CDs with a high quantum yield from banana peel waste with a low-temperature oxidation methodology. Furthermore, this photophysical study gave us insight into CD's excitation wavelength independent emission nature, aggregation behaviour and solvent-induced tunable emission behaviour. Interestingly the fluorescence emission maximum is independent of the excitation wavelength in various solvents (in both polar and non polar), in contrast to the majority of previous literature studies. These novel properties of CDs further utilised to develop bioimaging and organic acid sensing applications.

Fresh, ripe yellow bananas (Musa acuminata) were purchased at the local market of Chennai, Tamilnadu, India. Chemicals like acetone, phosphoric acid (4%), trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), pentanoic acid (PA), heptanoic acid (HA), octanoic acid (OA) and polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) were purchased from Avra, Sigma Aldrich, Merck and Fischer scientific. All the solvents used in the experiments were spectroscopic grade.

Steady-state absorption, steady-state emission, dynamic light scattering, time-resolved fluorescence and quantum yield measurements were carried out during the course of this investigation followed by standard procedures. The instrumental and experimental details have been given in supporting information (SI) 1.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell Culture, cytotoxicity assay and fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The 3T3 cells (Mouse fibroblast cells) were procured from National Center for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India and grown in Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium. The culture medium was supplemented with 10% heat inactivated fetal bovine serum, 1% L-glutamine and 1% Penicilin-Streptomycin antibiotic solution. The cells were maintained at 37 ˚C with 5% CO2 in a humidified CO2 incubator (Forma Steri-Cycle CO2 incubator, Thermo Scientific, USA).

The biocompatibility of the CDs were checked by cytotoxicity analysis by monitoring cell death, to explore its biological applications. The cytotoxicity of CDs was assessed using 3-[4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) based colorimetric assay on 3T3 cells [38]. In brief, 5 × 103 cells were seeded per well on a 96 well plate and left to attach overnight. The cells were treated and incubated for 24 h with diluted concentrations of CDs. After the experimental period, 10 µL of MTT (5 mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated for 3 h. The formed formazan crystals were dissolved in the stock solution containing 20% (w/v) of SDS in 50% dimethylformamide (v/v). The absorbance of each well was measured at 560 nm on an ELISA plate reader (Berthold Technologies, Germany). The cell viability was calculated with respect to the media control, and the dose–response curve was plotted against CDs concentrations.

For fluorescence imaging, approximately 2 × 105 3T3 cells were seeded per well on a 6 well plate and allowed overnight to attach. Then the cells were treated with 100 µL of CDs for 2 h at 37 ˚C. The cells were washed twice with Dulbecco’s phosphate buffer saline (DPBS), and images were obtained using a Nikon Eclipse Ti Fluorescence Microscope (Nikon, Japan). The GFP/FITC filter cube with 480/30 excitation filter, was used to obtain the green fluorescent images of the 3T3 cells.The brightfield images of carbon dot-NaCl crystals were also taken using the same microscope.

A facile and single-step oxidation method was developed to create excitation wavelength independent emissive phosphorous (P) doped CDs from banana peel waste without using the high-temperature hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). From previous literature, it has been shown that P doped CDs can be possible to synthesize at low temperatures [39,40]. The current synthesis procedure was suitably modified according to the previous reports, where excitation wavelength independent emissive CDs were synthesized from sucrose [40,41]. CDs were synthesized from ripe banana peel waste with one pot oxidation methodology using phosphoric acid heating at 85 °C for 30 min without post-treatment chromatographic purification or dialysis. The peel extract and phosphoric acid were chosen 1:2 proportion, as shown below.

The ripe banana peel extract was prepared according to our previous report [4]. The pale banana peel extract solution turned dark brown and finally black during oxidation at 85 °C. The reaction mixture was neutralized with NaOH (checked with pH paper) at room temperature after the completion of the reaction. Dichloromethane was used to extract the organic component and subsequently remove the aqueous phase. The organic extracted medium in dichloromethane was shown to have a bright yellow emission under UV 365 nm (Scheme 1), confirming the formation of CDs. Unlike most literature reports, these bright yellow emissive CDs are excitation independent in nature (Fig. S1). Sodium sulfate was used to remove the water fraction from dichloromethane extracted organic media. Powder CDs were obtained by evaporation of dichloromethane under low pressure.

CDs prepared from the ripe banana peelare spherical, monodispersed, and have a limited size distribution below 10 nm, as observed in the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (Fig. 1 A). The HRTEM image of banana peel extract was also taken to understand the particle size of the starting materials (Fig. S2). This image indicates that the peel extract had a much bigger particle size and heterogeneous size distribution. The lattice fringes (0.35 nm) obtained in the CDs are close to the graphite interlayer spacing (002) [40,42,43] (Fig. 1A inset). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to determine the crystallographic structure of CDs. These CDs have a broad peak at ∼ 23° in their XRD pattern (Fig. 1B), which corresponds to a d-spacing value, i.e. 0.37 nm. The lattice fringes determined from the HRTEM match well with the d-spacing value obtained from XRD. This d-spacing value (0.37 nm) is close to 0.33 nm, corresponding to bulk graphite's (002) plane [41]. This XRD pattern and the lattice fringes obtained from the current study were well consistent with previous literature on CDs obtained from a different source and methodology [40,41]. The composition of CDs is interpreted by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDAX) study. The EDAX spectrum of the CDs (recorded on Cu-grid/TEM grid) (Fig. 1C) shows the presence of carbon, oxygen and phosphor atoms. The current result of EDAX matches well with previous reports of P doped CDs [44]. FTIR study was used to understand the chemical functional groups present in the CDs. Fig. 1D shows the FTIR spectra of CDs where the broad peak centred at 3434 cm−1 indicates O–H stretching frequency [4]. The peak at 3105 cm−1 is due to sp2 (–C–H), and the peaks at 2926 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 are due to asymmetric and symmetric stretching of sp3 (–C–H), respectively [40,45].

The strong peak at 1653 cm−1 is due to conjugated carbonyl functional groups, and the peak at 1528 cm−1 is due to the C=C stretching frequency of the furfural functional group [4]. The peaks in the region of 1390 to 1000 cm−1 are due to C-O stretching frequency [4]. This stretching frequency can also be assigned to phosphorus and phospho-carbonaceous found in phosphoric acid activated carbon [40,45]. The prominent shoulder peak at 1162 cm−1 (Fig. 1D) can be attributed to hydrogen-bonded P=O groups from phosphates or polyphosphates or to the C–O stretching vibration of P–O–C (aromatic) [40,46]. The identification of functional groups from the FTIR study correlates well with EDAX result. To understand the nature and origin of this excitation wavelength independent emission of CDs, detailed photophysical studies were carried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysics of excitation wavelength independent emissive CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solution of CDs was prepared in dichloromethane as it was used to extract CDs from the reaction media. The formation of CDs was evidenced by the formation of a new absorption band at ∼ 360 nm in dichloromethane (Fig. S3) with a yellow emission. The starting material, i.e. banana peel extract, did not show any emission, as evidenced by the excitation-emission matrix fluorescence (EEMF) graph as shown in Fig. S4. Furthermore, CDs show a solvent dependent absorption and emission behaviour with different solvent polarities (Fig. 2 ). It shows reasonable solubility in most non-polar, weakly-polar and polar solvents. The solubility of CDs in different solvents indicates that both hydrophilic and hydrophobic moieties are present in their structure. In non-polar and weak polar solvents, the absorbance maximum appears to be ∼ 362 nm (Fig. 2A). The band intensity gradually decreases with solvent polarity, and a weaker absorption band appears at 422 nm in polar solvents, including acetonitrile, 1-butanol, dimethyl sulfoxide, methanol and water (Fig. 2A). The absorbance ratio of the 422 and 362 nm bands (A422/362) increases from ∼ 0.32 in cyclohexane to ∼ 0.83 in water. The 362 and 422 nm absorption bands are associated with the two different absorbing species resulting in different emissions, respectively (Fig. 2B and C). These observations indicate that two types of chromophores are present within the CDs. Interestingly the chromophore of CDs absorbing at 362 nm is predominant in weakly polar or non-polar solvents, along with a minor contribution from 422 nm, giving yellow emission (Fig. 2B). In contrast, the chromophore of CDs absorbing at 422 nm is predominant in polar solvents with a minor contribution from 362 nm, giving green emission (Fig. 2C). Furthermore, the shifting of the emission peak with changing solvent polarity indicates that the emitting chromophore of CDsis susceptible to interactions in various solvent environments. As a result, depending on the kind of solvents, the type of interaction of CDs could vary from H-bonding to dipole–dipole interaction. So it is extremely challenging to determine the definite chemical structure of CDs and a distinct core and its surfaces.

Furthermore, we attempted to draw a correlation between the change in the emission wavelength of CDs and with change in the solvent polarity (Table S1). We found that the emission wavelength varies linearly with an increase in solvent polarity index (Fig. 2D). These CDs also show excitation wavelength-independent emission in most solvents, including cyclohexane, tetrachloromethane, dichloromethane, acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide, 1-butanol and methanol (Fig. S5). In solvents like cyclohexane, tetrachloromethane, dichloromethane, acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide, 1-butanol and methanol, either of the emissions show much higher intensity with respect to the other solvents. In solvents like ethyl acetate, both emissions show almost equal intensity (
I
e x 422
e m 520
/
I
e x 362
e m 555
= 0.72). If we examine the excitation spectrum (Fig. 3 ) and compare them with the absorption spectrum, we can see that the excitation spectrums also consist of two bands. The predominant excitation maximum is at 362 nm, and a small band at 422 nm in the excitation spectrum of weakly polar or non-polar solvents. This is well identical to its absorption spectrum. Likewise, the excitation maximum at 422 nm (predominant) and a small band at 362 nm in the excitation spectrum of polar solvents like methanol, dimethylsulfoxide, 1-butanol and water is well identical to that in the absorption spectrum. The excitation spectrums of all solvents did not show any new bands.

EEMF measurement is performed for CDs in polar and non-polar solvents to have an overall change in excitation and emission wavelength (Fig. S6). The maximum emission intensity appears at ∼ 510 nm in polar solvents, and the corresponding excitation maximum appears at ∼ 422 nm. Similarly, in non-polar or weakly polar solvents, the maximum emission intensity appears at ∼ 560 nm, and the corresponding excitation maximum appears at ∼ 360 nm. Fig. S6 clearly indicates that CDs' excitation and emission wavelength were changed in different solvents ranging from non-polar to polar media. However, close observation reveals that all the emission profiles combine a shorter wavelength emission (λmax ∼ 510 nm) and a longer wavelength emission (λmax ∼ 560 nm). The time-resolved experiments further confirm the dual emission nature of CDs (vide infra).

The following observations were made during the solvatochromic investigation of CDs in different solvents: (1) the emission spectra were excitation-independent in each case, i.e., the position of the emission peak did not change with a change in excitation wavelength, (2) A blueshift in the emission spectra was observed at the same excitation wavelength (λex = 360 nm and 440 nm) with an increase in solvent polarity indicates negative solvatochromism. These two findings suggest that these CDsare excitation-independent but solvent-dependent. Owing to their tuneable excitation independent emission properties (yellow and green) and high stability in different solvents, these CDs can be used as a probe to identify hydrophilic or hydrophobic environments for different applications. The full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the CD's emission spectrum is often more than 100 nm, owing to the wide spectrum caused by different particle sizes or heterogeneity [33,47,48]. Furthermore, the broad luminescence band (high FWHM) limits the practical usage of CDs in biological or LED applications. Unlikely previous reports, the FWHM of current yellow emissive CDs in dichloromethane has been ∼ 80 nm, demonstrating good colour purity (Fig. 2B).

The fluorescence lifetime of CDs was measured to understand the lifetime of the excited process. The fluorescence decay in different solvents was recorded with 350 nm and 450 nm excitation and emission at 560 nm and 500 nm (Fig. S7). The CDs exhibit biexponential decay in all solvents (Table 1 ). At lifetime of
λ
ex = 350
em = 560
, the average lifetime (τavg) increases from non-polar to polar solvents. Additionally, the longer lifetime of ∼ 4 ns of relative amplitude decreases from 90% to 65% and the shorter lifetime component of ∼ 1 ns of relative amplitude increases from 10% to 35% for non-polar to polar solvents. As these CDs have both hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups, CDs are expected to form aggregates because of their partial insolubility in different solvents of different polarities. Hence a uniform trend was not observed in lifetime studies in different solvents. Similarly, the time-resolved studies at
λ
ex = 450
em = 500
also did not follow any trend for the different polarities of the solvents. This might be due to CD aggregates, which will be examined later in more detail in this article.

These CDs show emissions in the variety of solvents, from polar to non-polar, with a high quantum yield value. Hence this feature can be suitable for many applications instead of using fluorescence dyes. The maximum emission quantum yield of CDs appears ∼ 0.3 in polar solvents like 1-butanol, methanol and dimethyl sulfoxide. An increase in the radiative rate constant and a decrease in the non-radiative decay constant results in a high quantum yield in polar solvents (Table 2 ). However, the quantum yield value of CDs was decreased to ∼ 0.2 in water. Probably, due to low solubility, CDs form aggregates in water. The previously reported excitation independent P doped CDs had low quantum yield with a high-temperature synthesis method depicted in Table S2. However, the mixed doped (N, P or S) strategy reported a few high quantum yields of CDs with post purification methods [13,44]. The mild graphitization process at low temperatures incorporates the surface defect to the present CDs uniformly to yield excitation wavelength-independent emission with high quantum yield.

In order to understand the aggregation aspect of CDs in the solvents, dynamic light scattering (DLS) study was carried out (Fig. 4 ). CDs show particle sizes (number average) less than 10 nm for non-polar and moderately polar solvents like cyclohexane, carbon tetrachloride, dichloromethane and 1-butanol. This result corresponds well with the particle size shown in the HRTEM image. The particle size was increased in solvents like ethyl acetate, acetonitrile, methanol and water. This could be due to CDs' less solubility, resulting in aggregation in those polar solvents. A significant aggregation of CDs occurs in the aqueous medium attributed to large particle size (Fig. 4). Earlier reports show that excess resonance energy transfer in the CDs aggregates leads to emission quenching [50,51]. Therefore, an aggregation caused by loss of fluorescence is more prominent in water as compared to other polar protic media. The size of the CDs influences the surface density of emissive traps and band-gap levels, which in turn influences their optical characteristics [52]. So, the formation of CDs aggregates correlates well with the CD’s dual emissions in different solvent polarities. In general, a large stock shift can be found for the system having charge transfer donor–acceptor moiety or extended conjugations [41]. A large Stokes shifted emission (excitation maximum 360 nm and emission maximum 560 nm ≈ 9606 cm−1) in non-polar and weak polar media of CDs infers that the aggregation of CDs could be the primary region. Because of the aggregation-induced energy stabilization of the excited state, the stokes shift is very large. Many previous literature reports observed a similar magnitude of large stokes shift in the dyes and nanoparticle aggregates [41,53,54]. From the above discussion; it can be rationalized that these CDs in weakly polar and non-polar solvents have smaller particle sizes that give yellow emissions. Likewise, same CDs have larger particle sizes in polar solvents, giving green emissions.

In general, the CDs in solution are emissive; however, the loss of fluorescence intensity in the solid-state is well known as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [4,55,56]. This is the major obstacle to CDs being used in solid-state applications. Similarly to previous reports, in the current research, no emission property was observed in the solid-state of CDs after they dried out from solvent (dichloromethane) on a glass slide (Fig. 5 ). The emission loss of CDs due to aggregation can be prevented by embedding CDs into the polar and non-polar host matrix, as shown in Fig. 5A. The bright green and yellow emission was obtained by embedding CDs in NaCl and (poly(methyl methacrylate (PMMA) matrix (Fig. 5B and D). Both solid state matrix synthesis procedures were presented in SI section 1.1. The EEMF graph (Fig. 5C) was not shown any emission for CDs in the solid state. Both CDs-NaCl and CDs-PMMA have an exponentially decreased absorbance from lower to higher wavelength with an absorption shoulder at ∼ 360 nm and ∼ 450 nm (Fig. 5E).

The CDs in PMMA film emit a yellow emission at ∼ 550 nm with excitation at 450 nm (Fig. 5E). In the NaCl host matrix, the CDs show a green emission at ∼ 520 nm with excitation at 350 nm (Fig. 5E). The EEMF graph indicates that the excitation and emission maxima of CDs were changed in the PMMA and NaCl host matrix as compared to the CDs in dichloromethane. The change in emission behaviour of CDs in the solid matrix could be due to the change in the dielectric constant of the media, well reported in the previous literature [4,57]. Fluorescence microscope images of CDs-NaCl crystal further confirm the green emission, as shown in Fig. S8. The digital images supported similar results under UV 365 nm. The parameters associated with the decay for both systems are shown in Fig. 5G and Table S3. The CDs-PMMA had a longer average lifetime (τavg ≈ 4.15 ns) as compared to CDs- NaCl crystals (τavg ≈ 1.92 ns). Hence ACQ of CDs in the solid state can be overcome by embedding CDs into different host matrices.

Photostability is an important parameter of CDs for any possible application. The light irradiation studies were carried out for this excitation independent emissive CDs in dichloromethane with the excitation of 360 nm and emission at 560 nm. CDs are reasonably stable on light irradiation with respect to time, as shown in Fig. S9.

The organic acids chosen for the sensing study were trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), pentanoic acid (PA), heptanoic acid (HA) and octanoic acid (OA)) of concentration 215 μM, as shown in Figs. S10 and S11. The interaction of organic acids with CDs in dichloromethane resulted in decreased fluorescence intensity. For TFA, the fluorescence intensity was decreased to the maximum for CDs with a red-shift. (Fig. 6D and S11).

Additionally, the control experiments for steady-state absorbance and emission studies of CDs with TFA were carried out to understand the acid response with CDs by increasing the concentration of TFA from 4 μM to 2 mM (Fig. 6A and S12). The absorption band at 360 nm was initially red-shifted by the addition of TFA up to 200 μM concentration. Afterwards, the absorption band showed a blue shift at 360 nm (Fig. 6A) with increased TFA concentration. Very similar to absorbance spectra, steady-state emission with excitation of 360 nm was also initially shifted from ∼ 560 nm to ∼ 592 nm. Then nearly 140 nm blue shift was observed with the TFA concentration exceeding 200 μM (Fig. 6C). A similar shift in the emission maximum was observed for 430 nm excitation (Fig. S12). The ratiometric emission intensity of I455/I560 and I500/I590 with the excitation of 360 nm and 430 nm indicated a gradual increase in emission intensity, as shown in Fig. 6B. So it was concluded that the emission intensity of CDs was decreased, and a red-shift was observed from 560 nm to 590 nm for CDs-TFA.

Furthermore, TFA was shown a change in excitation and emission maximum in the EEMF plot (Fig. S10). The change in the outer surface of CDs during the protonation by TFA was most likely responsible for the red-shift of absorption and emission spectrum. In contrast, PA, HA and OA do not produce any red-shift in the CD′s emission spectra; and a small loss of the emission intensity of CDs was noticed (Fig. S11 and Fig. 6D). We presumed that the interaction of an acid with CDs opens up additional non-radiative channels, resulting in decreased fluorescence intensity of CDs. Furthermore, when the base, i.e. triethylamine (TEA), was added, the weak orange emissive CDs-TFA solution was changed back to bright yellow emission (Fig. 7 ). The process was quasi-reversible as evidenced by steady-state (Fig. 7A and Fig. S13) and time-resolved studies (Fig. 7B).

The protonation and deprotonation of the chromophore present in CDs appear to cause a change in emission behaviour in dichloromethane [58]. TFA is a strong acid; hence a fast protonation was observed for the chromophore present on CDs, producing a huge loss of emission intensity for CDs. In contrast, other organic acids (like OA, HA, PA) are weak acids. So the slow protonation results in a slight decrease in emission intensity for CDs. The change in emission intensity and average lifetime with the addition of TEA and TFA were monitored at an excitation of 360 nm and emission at 560 nm (Fig. 7C and Fig. S13). The switching in emission intensity and emission lifetime can be repeated up to several times, as shown in Fig. 7C. The digital image of the change in emission wavelength of CDs after adding TFA and TEA under UV 365 nm (Fig. 7C) showed that TFA could be detected with the naked eye using these CDs, and this can be utilized for multiple cycles. Fig. 7D indicates the change in emission wavelength after several successive TFA and TEA addition in the CDs solution. The emission wavelengths were unchanged and switched to the original wavelength after several cycles of acid-base treatments.

Usually, CDs with longer-wavelength emission are synthesized in organic solvents. Often, these materials have limited solubility in water due to their hydrophobic nature, which poses substantial limitations in biosensing and bioimaging applications [8,59]. As a result, the development of high-efficiency, longer wavelength emitting CDs with amphiphilic nature and their fluorescent-based biosensing applications remain a major priority for the current scenario.

In the current study, the prepared CDs are amphiphilic and fairly water-soluble. These CDs show excitation independent, bright green emission with a fluorescence quantum yield of 22%. The cell viability with the CDs treatment was demonstrated in 3T3 fibroblast cells to evaluate the plausible cytotoxicity, as shown in Fig. 8 A. The cell viability data infers the negligible cytotoxicity of the prepared CDs, indicating their possible biological applications. Further, the fluorescence imaging potential of the same CDs was checked in 3T3 cells. The CDs were imaged in the FITC filter, as depicted in Fig. 8B.

A straightforward low-temperature oxidation method has been developed to produce amphiphilic CDs with excitation wavelength-independent emission from ripe banana peel waste. The particle size of CDs was increased due to insolubility from non-polar to polar solvents with a change in emission colour from yellow to green. Hence these CDs can be used to sense hydrophilic and hydrophobic solvent environments with changes in emission colour. Detailed photophysical studies further understand the origin of tunable excitation wavelength-independent emissions from these CDs. The quantum yields of these CDs were 9–32% in different solvents for different emissions. The CDs are reasonably photostable. The ACQ effect of CDs in the solid state can be overcome by using ionic (NaCl salts) and polymer host matrix (PMMA). These CDs can detect organic acids with the loss of fluorescence intensity. Trifluoroacetic acid can be detected more promisingly by changing the emission colour from yellow to orange under UV 365 nm with the naked eye and reversibly switching for several cycles. These excitation wavelengths independent emissive CDs were potentially utilized in bioimaging applications in 3T3 fibroblast cells and had negligible cytotoxicity. This finding expands the scope of understanding the origin of excitation wavelength independent emission of CDs and paves the way for potential application in organic acid sensing and bioimaging applications.
Absorbance, excitation, emission and EEMF spectra of CDs, fluorescence microscope images of CDs-NaCl and TEM image of banana peel extract.

Swayam Prakash: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Project administration, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Saugata Sahu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Software, Writing – original draft. Bamadeb Patra: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ashok Kumar Mishra: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Department of Chemistry and SAIF, IIT Madras, for the instrumental facilities. The authors thank Dr E. Prasad, Department of Chemistry, IIT Madras, for providing the DLS instrument facility. The authors thank IIT Madras for their research fellowship. SP grateful to Dr Anand Kumar Sahu for the proofreading.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
